--- title: "Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part II " subtitle: "Answering Marketing Questions about GA4 Data" description: "Following up on Par I, this document shows what marketing questions can be asked for GA4 Data and how to answer them" categories: [bigrquery, qs2, dplyr, ggplot2, gt, gtExtra, tidyverse] order: 3 author: "Jae Jung" date: today format: html: theme: light: flatly dark: darkly toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: right-body toc-title: "Contents" toc-expand: true code-fold: show code-copy: true code-tools: true highlight-style: github fig-width: 9 fig-height: 5 fig-align: center df-print: paged number-sections: true smooth-scroll: true embed-resources: true callout-appearance: minimal execute: warning: false message: false cache: true freeze: auto --- # Setup {.unnumbered} ## Load Packages ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # install.packages(c("vctrs", "dplyr"), repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org") library(bigrquery) library(DBI) library(arrow) library(duckdb) library(duckplyr) library(tidyr) library(tidyverse) ``` ::: ## Connect to BigQuery ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Configuration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── GCP_PROJECT <- "ga4-bigquery-cppmsdm" # <-- change this BQ_PROJECT <- "bigquery-public-data" BQ_DATASET <- "ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce" # Authenticate (opens browser on first run; token cached in ~/.cache/gargle) # bq_auth() # uncomment if not yet authed # bq_user() # Open a DBI connection to BigQuery bq_con <- dbConnect( bigrquery::bigquery(), project = BQ_PROJECT, dataset = BQ_DATASET, billing = GCP_PROJECT ) # Confirm tables are visible dbListTables(bq_con) ``` ::: ## Pull Data from BigQuery → Single Parquet File ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Run this chunk ONCE. It saves data locally. ─────────────────────────────── dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE) query_result <- bq_project_query( GCP_PROJECT, "SELECT event_date, event_timestamp, event_name, event_params, user_pseudo_id, device, geo, traffic_source, ecommerce, items, user_properties FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*` WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210107'" ) # A tibble data residing in memory ga4_raw <- query_result |> bq_table_download( bigint = "numeric", # Convert INT64 to R numeric (sufficient for most analyses) page_size = 1000 # Download in batches of 10k rows ) # Save once after BigQuery download # install.packages("qs2") library(qs2) # Save qs_save(ga4_raw, "data/ga4_raw.qs2") # Disconnect from BigQuery — we don't need it anymore dbDisconnect(bq_con) ``` ::: ## Load the data to RAM ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(qs2) # Create or load an Arrow table ga4_qs <- qs_read("data/ga4_raw.qs2") ``` ::: ::: callout-note ### `qs` Package **qs** stands for **Quick Serialization**. It is a drop-in replacement for `saveRDS()`/`readRDS()` that uses a more efficient compression algorithm (**ZSTD**) instead of R's default gzip, making it: - **2-5x faster** to save and load - **Smaller file sizes** than RDS - **Preserves all R objects** exactly, including nested lists, data frames, and complex structures It was designed specifically for the use case of **caching large R objects locally** — which is exactly what we need for GA4 data. `qs2` is the newer, updated version with further performance improvements. ::: # Part 3: Marketing Analytics in Practice {#part3} > Now we assemble all the pieces into complete marketing analyses. Each section below builds an analytical table from the GA4 data (7 days) using the rectangling tools we have learned, then produces a visualisation and interprets it. ## Building the Master Analytical Table Rather than re-running the pipelines for every analysis, we assemble one wide, analysis-ready table that joins the flattened structs, flattened event_params, and e-commerce data: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Step 1: Flatten struct columns ──────────────────────────────────────────── ga4_structs <- ga4_qs |> filter(event_name == "purchase") |> select( event_date, event_name, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, device, geo, traffic_source ) |> unnest_wider(device, names_sep = "_") |> unnest_wider(geo, names_sep = "_") |> unnest_wider(traffic_source, names_sep = "_") names(ga4_structs) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "event_date" "event_name" [3] "event_timestamp" "user_pseudo_id" [5] "device_category" "device_mobile_brand_name" [7] "device_mobile_model_name" "device_mobile_marketing_name" [9] "device_mobile_os_hardware_model" "device_operating_system" [11] "device_operating_system_version" "device_vendor_id" [13] "device_advertising_id" "device_language" [15] "device_is_limited_ad_tracking" "device_time_zone_offset_seconds" [17] "device_web_info" "geo_continent" [19] "geo_sub_continent" "geo_country" [21] "geo_region" "geo_city" [23] "geo_metro" "traffic_source_medium" [25] "traffic_source_name" "traffic_source_source" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Step 2: Flatten event_params ────────────────────────────────────────────── ga4_params <- ga4_qs |> select( event_date, event_name, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, event_params ) |> unnest_longer(event_params) |> unnest_wider(event_params) |> unnest_wider(value, names_sep = "_") |> unnest_wider(value_1) |> mutate( param_value = coalesce( string_value, as.character(int_value), as.character(float_value), as.character(double_value) ) ) |> select( event_date, event_name, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, key, param_value ) |> pivot_wider( names_from = key, values_from = param_value, values_fn = first ) # Save qs_save(ga4_params, "data/ga4_params.qs2") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create or load an Arrow table ga4_params <- qs_read("data/ga4_params.qs2") glimpse(ga4_params) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 245,209 Columns: 37 $ event_date "20210102", "20210102", "20210102", "20210102", … $ event_name "page_view", "user_engagement", "first_visit", "… $ event_timestamp 1.609568e+15, 1.609568e+15, 1.609568e+15, 1.6095… $ user_pseudo_id "1005484.1092567297", "1005484.1092567297", "100… $ gclsrc NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ debug_mode "1", "1", NA, "1", NA, "1", NA, "1", NA, NA, "1"… $ page_title "YouTube | Shop by Brand | Google Merchandise St… $ engagement_time_msec "26", "11872", NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, "4", NA, NA, … $ session_engaged "1", "1", "1", "0", NA, "0", NA, "1", NA, "1", "… $ ga_session_number "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "2", "1", "1", "1"… $ engaged_session_event "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", NA, "1", "1", "1",… $ ga_session_id "2718913892", "2718913892", "2718913892", "27189… $ gclid NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ page_location "https://shop.googlemerchandisestore.com/Google+… $ all_data NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ entrances NA, NA, NA, "1", NA, "1", NA, NA, NA, NA, "1", N… $ clean_event NA, NA, NA, "gtm.js", NA, "gtm.js", NA, NA, NA, … $ page_referrer NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ medium NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ campaign NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ source NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ percent_scrolled NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ currency NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ unique_search_term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ search_term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ dclid NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ outbound NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ link_domain NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ link_url NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ transaction_id NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ shipping_tier NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ promotion_name NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ coupon NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ tax NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ value NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ payment_type NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Step 3: Flatten ecommerce struct ────────────────────────────────────────── ga4_ecomm <- ga4_qs |> select(event_date, event_name, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, ecommerce) |> unnest_wider(ecommerce, names_sep = "_") # ── Step 4: Join all together ───────────────────────────────────────────────── ga4_master <- ga4_structs |> left_join( ga4_params, by = c("event_date", "event_name", "event_timestamp", "user_pseudo_id") ) |> left_join( ga4_ecomm, by = c("event_date", "event_name", "event_timestamp", "user_pseudo_id") ) |> # Convert event_timestamp (microseconds since Unix epoch) to datetime mutate( event_dt = as.POSIXct( as.numeric(event_timestamp) / 1e6, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "America/Los_Angeles" # GA4 obfuscated dataset is in US/Pacific ), event_date = ymd(event_date) ) glimpse(ga4_master) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 144 Columns: 71 $ event_date 2021-01-02, 2021-01-02, 2021-01-02,… $ event_name "purchase", "purchase", "purchase", … $ event_timestamp 1.609619e+15, 1.609558e+15, 1.609615… $ user_pseudo_id "1055969.5872512303", "3024189.61016… $ device_category "mobile", "mobile", "mobile", "mobil… $ device_mobile_brand_name "Samsung", "Apple", "Xiaomi", "Apple… $ device_mobile_model_name "", "iPhone", "", "iPh… $ device_mobile_marketing_name "", "", "", " NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ device_operating_system "Web", "iOS", "Android", "Web", "And… $ device_operating_system_version "", "iOS 14.2", "", "<… $ device_vendor_id NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ device_advertising_id NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ device_language "en-us", "zh", NA, "en-us", NA, NA, … $ device_is_limited_ad_tracking "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", … $ device_time_zone_offset_seconds NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ device_web_info [["Chrome", "87.0"]], [["Safari", "… $ geo_continent "Americas", "Americas", "Americas", … $ geo_sub_continent "Northern America", "Northern Americ… $ geo_country "United States", "United States", "U… $ geo_region "Florida", "Colorado", "Pennsylvania… $ geo_city "(not set)", "(not set)", "Philadelp… $ geo_metro "(not set)", "(not set)", "(not set)… $ traffic_source_medium "referral", "organic", "organic", "<… $ traffic_source_name "(referral)", "(organic)", "(organic… $ traffic_source_source "shop.googlemerchandisestore.com", "… $ gclsrc NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ debug_mode "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "… $ page_title "Checkout Confirmation", "Checkout C… $ engagement_time_msec "5", "11", "1", "4", "4", "1", "7", … $ session_engaged "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "… $ ga_session_number "2", "4", "1", "1", "1", "1", "3", "… $ engaged_session_event "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "1", "… $ ga_session_id "5298604884", "7859325572", "7961433… $ gclid NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ page_location "https://shop.googlemerchandisestore… $ all_data NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ entrances NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ clean_event NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ page_referrer "https://shop.googlemerchandisestore… $ medium "referral", "referral", "organic", "… $ term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ campaign "(referral)", "(referral)", "(organi… $ source "shop.googlemerchandisestore.com", "… $ percent_scrolled NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ currency "USD", "USD", "USD", "USD", "USD", "… $ unique_search_term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ search_term NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ dclid NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ outbound NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ link_domain NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ link_url NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ transaction_id "4256792636", "2073562964", NA, "737… $ shipping_tier "FedEx Ground", "FedEx Ground", NA, … $ promotion_name "reach new heights", NA, "reach new … $ coupon "7365791999877465472", "906187848818… $ tax "0", "1.17", NA, "4.98", "4.46", "1.… $ value "7", "13", NA, "48", "41.6", "10.5",… $ payment_type "Pay with credit card", "Pay with cr… $ ecommerce_total_item_quantity 1, 1, NA, 1, 4, 3, 1, 27, 6, 4, 4, 6… $ ecommerce_purchase_revenue_in_usd 7, 13, 0, 48, 42, 11, 63, 87, 21, 16… $ ecommerce_purchase_revenue 7, 13, NA, 48, 42, 11, 63, 87, 21, 1… $ ecommerce_refund_value_in_usd NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ ecommerce_refund_value NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ ecommerce_shipping_value_in_usd NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ ecommerce_shipping_value NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … $ ecommerce_tax_value_in_usd 0, 1, NA, 5, 4, 2, 6, 0, 0, 13, 4, 0… $ ecommerce_tax_value 0, 1, NA, 5, 4, 2, 6, 0, 0, 13, 4, 0… $ ecommerce_unique_items 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, … $ ecommerce_transaction_id "792636", "562964", "(not set)", "70… $ event_dt 2021-01-02 12:17:19, 2021-01-01 19:… ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cat( "Master table dimensions:", nrow(ga4_master), "rows ×", ncol(ga4_master), "columns\n", "Date range:", format(min(ga4_master$event_date)), "to", format(max(ga4_master$event_date)), "\n", "Unique users:", n_distinct(ga4_master$user_pseudo_id), "\n", "Unique event types:", n_distinct(ga4_master$event_name), "\n" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Master table dimensions: 144 rows × 71 columns Date range: 2021-01-01 to 2021-01-07 Unique users: 136 Unique event types: 1 ``` ::: ::: ## Analysis 1: Traffic Source Performance {#traffic-analysis} **Marketing Question**: Which channels are driving the most engaged traffic? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} traffic_analysis <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "user_engagement") |> mutate( # Clean and standardise channel labels medium = case_when( is.na(traffic_source_medium) ~ "(none)", traffic_source_medium == "(none)" ~ "direct", traffic_source_medium == "organic" ~ "organic search", traffic_source_medium %in% c("cpc", "ppc", "paid") ~ "paid search", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "email") ~ "email", str_detect( traffic_source_medium, "social|referral" ) ~ traffic_source_medium, TRUE ~ traffic_source_medium ) ) |> group_by(medium) |> summarise( sessions = n(), unique_users = n_distinct(user_pseudo_id), .groups = "drop" ) |> # Join in engagement and conversion metrics from full event stream left_join( ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> mutate( medium = case_when( is.na(traffic_source_medium) ~ "(none)", traffic_source_medium == "(none)" ~ "direct", traffic_source_medium == "organic" ~ "organic search", traffic_source_medium %in% c("cpc", "ppc", "paid") ~ "paid search", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "email") ~ "email", TRUE ~ traffic_source_medium ) ) |> group_by(medium, user_pseudo_id) |> summarise(.groups = "drop") |> left_join( ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "purchase") |> distinct(user_pseudo_id) |> mutate(converted = TRUE), by = "user_pseudo_id" ) |> group_by(medium) |> summarise( users_who_purchased = sum(!is.na(converted)), .groups = "drop" ), by = "medium" ) |> mutate( conversion_rate = users_who_purchased / unique_users, sessions_pct = sessions / sum(sessions) ) |> arrange(desc(sessions)) traffic_analysis ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ── Panel A: Session Volume by Channel ──────────────────────────────────────── library(scales) p1 <- traffic_analysis |> filter(sessions > 10) |> mutate(medium = fct_reorder(medium, sessions)) |> ggplot(aes(x = medium, y = sessions, fill = medium)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = comma(sessions)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous( labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)) ) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") + labs( title = "Sessions by Traffic Channel", subtitle = "Jan 1–7, 2021 · Google Merchandise Store", x = NULL, y = "Sessions" ) # ── Panel B: Conversion Rate by Channel ─────────────────────────────────────── p2 <- traffic_analysis |> filter(sessions > 10, !is.na(conversion_rate)) |> mutate(medium = fct_reorder(medium, conversion_rate)) |> ggplot(aes(x = medium, y = conversion_rate, fill = medium)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = percent(conversion_rate, accuracy = 0.1)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous( labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22)) ) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") + labs( title = "Purchase Conversion Rate by Channel", subtitle = "Users who completed a purchase ÷ users who started a session", x = NULL, y = "Conversion Rate" ) # Display side by side using patchwork if available, else stacked if (requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) { library(patchwork) p1 + p2 } else { print(p1) print(p2) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](hierarchical-data-II_files/figure-html/traffic-source-chart-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: callout-tip ## Marketing Interpretation - **Volume vs. quality**: The channel with the most sessions is not necessarily the best performer. Compare session volume against conversion rate to identify high-value channels. - **Direct traffic** often represents users who already know the brand — expect higher conversion rates. - **Organic search** volume indicates SEO health; low conversion from organic can signal a landing page or audience-fit problem. ::: ## Analysis 2: Device and Geographic Breakdown {#device-geo} **Marketing Question**: Where are users coming from, and on what devices? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Daily sessions by device category daily_device <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> count(event_date, device_category) |> mutate(device_category = str_to_title(device_category)) # Geographic distribution — top countries top_countries <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> count(geo_country, sort = TRUE) |> filter(!is.na(geo_country)) |> slice_head(n = 15) |> mutate( pct = n / sum(n), geo_country = fct_reorder(geo_country, n) ) # Browser share (for device-specific targeting decisions) browser_share <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> count(device_browser, device_category) |> filter(!is.na(device_browser)) |> group_by(device_category) |> mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |> slice_max(pct, n = 5) |> ungroup() ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} daily_device |> ggplot(aes( x = event_date, y = n, colour = device_category, group = device_category )) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) + scale_x_date(date_labels = "%a\n%b %d", date_breaks = "1 day") + scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) + scale_colour_manual( values = c( "Desktop" = "#4285F4", "Mobile" = "#34A853", "Tablet" = "#FBBC05" ) ) + labs( title = "Daily Sessions by Device Category", subtitle = "Jan 1–7, 2021 · Google Merchandise Store", x = NULL, y = "Sessions", colour = "Device" ) + theme(legend.position = "top") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(glue) top_countries |> ggplot(aes(x = geo_country, y = n, fill = pct)) + geom_col(width = 0.7) + geom_text( aes(label = glue("{comma(n)}\n({percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)})")), hjust = -0.1, size = 3, lineheight = 0.9 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous( labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.25)) ) + scale_fill_gradient( low = "#c6dbef", high = "#2171b5", labels = percent, name = "% of Sessions" ) + labs( title = "Sessions by Country (Top 15)", subtitle = "Jan 1–7, 2021 · Google Merchandise Store", x = NULL, y = "Sessions" ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} browser_share |> mutate(device_browser = fct_reorder(device_browser, pct)) |> ggplot(aes( x = device_category, y = device_browser, fill = pct, label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1) )) + geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.8) + geom_text(size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") + scale_fill_gradient( low = "#f7fbff", high = "#2171b5", labels = percent, name = "Share" ) + labs( title = "Browser Share by Device Category (Top 5 per Device)", subtitle = "Useful for QA testing priority decisions", x = "Device Category", y = "Browser" ) + theme(panel.grid = element_blank()) ``` ::: ## Analysis 3: Content Performance {#content-analysis} **Marketing Question**: Which pages attract the most traffic, and how do users navigate the site? This analysis requires extracting `page_location` from `event_params` — a direct application of the key-value pipeline we built in Part 2. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} page_views <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "page_view", !is.na(page_location)) |> mutate( # Parse the URL path from the full URL page_path = str_remove(page_location, "^https?://[^/]+"), page_path = str_remove(page_path, "\\?.*$"), # Remove query strings page_path = str_remove(page_path, "#.*$"), # Remove anchors page_path = if_else(page_path == "" | is.na(page_path), "/", page_path), # Categorise pages into sections for rolled-up analysis page_section = case_when( page_path == "/" ~ "Home", str_detect(page_path, "^/google\\+re|^/store|^/shop") ~ "Store", str_detect(page_path, "^/product") ~ "Product Pages", str_detect(page_path, "^/basket|^/cart") ~ "Cart", str_detect(page_path, "^/checkout") ~ "Checkout", str_detect(page_path, "^/signin|^/log") ~ "Sign In", str_detect(page_path, "^/brands") ~ "Brands", TRUE ~ "Other" ) ) # Top individual pages top_pages <- page_views |> count(page_path, page_section, sort = TRUE) |> slice_head(n = 20) # Page section rollup section_rollup <- page_views |> count(page_section, sort = TRUE) |> mutate( pct = n / sum(n), page_section = fct_reorder(page_section, n) ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} top_pages |> slice_head(n = 15) |> mutate( page_path = str_trunc(page_path, 45), page_path = fct_reorder(page_path, n) ) |> ggplot(aes(x = page_path, y = n, fill = page_section)) + geom_col(width = 0.7) + geom_text( aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous( labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)) ) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Section") + labs( title = "Top 15 Pages by Page Views", subtitle = "Jan 1–7, 2021 · Colour = site section", x = NULL, y = "Page Views" ) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](hierarchical-data-II_files/figure-html/top-pages-chart-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} section_rollup |> ggplot(aes(x = page_section, y = n, fill = page_section)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = glue("{comma(n)}\n{percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)}")), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5, lineheight = 0.9 ) + scale_y_continuous( labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2)) ) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Paired") + labs( title = "Page Views by Site Section", subtitle = "Rolled-up view using URL path categorisation", x = NULL, y = "Page Views" ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # What external pages referred traffic to the site? top_referrers <- ga4_master |> filter( event_name == "page_view", !is.na(page_referrer), page_referrer != "", !str_detect(page_referrer, "google\\.com/merchandise|merch\\.google") ) |> mutate( referrer_domain = str_extract(page_referrer, "(?<=://)[^/]+"), referrer_domain = str_remove(referrer_domain, "^www\\.") ) |> count(referrer_domain, sort = TRUE) |> filter(!is.na(referrer_domain)) |> slice_head(n = 10) top_referrers |> gt() |> tab_header( title = "Top 10 Referring Domains", subtitle = "External sites sending traffic to Google Merchandise Store" ) |> fmt_number(columns = n, decimals = 0) |> cols_label(referrer_domain = "Referring Domain", n = "Referrals") |> opt_stylize(style = 1) ``` ::: ## Analysis 4: E-commerce Funnel {#ecommerce-analysis} **Marketing Question**: Where in the purchase funnel are we losing customers? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Define funnel stages and their GA4 event names funnel_stages <- tibble( stage_order = 1:6, event_name = c( "view_item", "add_to_cart", "view_cart", "begin_checkout", "add_payment_info", "purchase" ), stage_label = c( "View Item", "Add to Cart", "View Cart", "Begin Checkout", "Add Payment Info", "Purchase" ) ) # Count unique users at each funnel stage funnel_data <- funnel_stages |> left_join( ga4_master |> filter(event_name %in% funnel_stages$event_name) |> group_by(event_name) |> summarise(users = n_distinct(user_pseudo_id), .groups = "drop"), by = "event_name" ) |> arrange(stage_order) |> mutate( users = replace_na(users, 0), top_users = max(users), pct_of_top = users / top_users, pct_prev = users / lag(users), # Drop-off to previous stage drop_off = 1 - pct_prev ) funnel_data |> select(stage_label, users, pct_of_top, pct_prev, drop_off) |> gt() |> tab_header( title = "Purchase Funnel Analysis", subtitle = "Unique users reaching each stage · Jan 1–7, 2021" ) |> fmt_number(columns = users, decimals = 0) |> fmt_percent(columns = c(pct_of_top, pct_prev, drop_off), decimals = 1) |> cols_label( stage_label = "Funnel Stage", users = "Users", pct_of_top = "% of Top Stage", pct_prev = "Retained from Prior", drop_off = "Drop-off Rate" ) |> data_color( columns = drop_off, method = "numeric", palette = c("#ffffff", "#f4433620"), na_color = "white" ) |> sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |> opt_stylize(style = 1) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} funnel_data |> mutate(stage_label = fct_inorder(stage_label)) |> ggplot(aes(x = stage_label, y = users)) + geom_col( aes(fill = pct_of_top), width = 0.75, show.legend = FALSE ) + geom_text( aes( label = glue( "{comma(users)} users\n({percent(pct_of_top, accuracy=0.1)})" ) ), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5, lineheight = 0.85, fontface = "bold" ) + # Annotate drop-off rates between bars geom_text( data = funnel_data |> filter(!is.na(drop_off)), aes( x = as.numeric(fct_inorder(stage_label)) - 0.5, y = max(funnel_data$users) * 0.55, label = glue("▼ {percent(drop_off, accuracy=1)}\ndrop-off") ), size = 3, colour = "#e53935", lineheight = 0.85 ) + scale_y_continuous( labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2)) ) + scale_fill_gradient(low = "#bbdefb", high = "#1565c0") + labs( title = "Purchase Funnel: Users by Stage", subtitle = "Jan 1–7, 2021 · Google Merchandise Store", x = NULL, y = "Unique Users" ) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(gt) # Revenue analysis using the flattened ecommerce struct revenue_daily <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "purchase") |> group_by(event_date) |> summarise( transactions = n(), total_revenue = sum( as.numeric(ecommerce_purchase_revenue_in_usd), na.rm = TRUE ), avg_order_value = mean( as.numeric(ecommerce_purchase_revenue_in_usd), na.rm = TRUE ), unique_buyers = n_distinct(user_pseudo_id), .groups = "drop" ) revenue_daily |> gt() |> tab_header( title = "Daily Revenue Summary", subtitle = "Derived from ecommerce struct (Pattern 1 unnesting)" ) |> fmt_date(columns = event_date, date_style = "day_m_year") |> fmt_number(columns = transactions, decimals = 0) |> fmt_currency(columns = c(total_revenue, avg_order_value), currency = "USD") |> fmt_number(columns = unique_buyers, decimals = 0) |> cols_label( event_date = "Date", transactions = "Transactions", total_revenue = "Revenue", avg_order_value = "Avg. Order Value", unique_buyers = "Unique Buyers" ) |> grand_summary_rows( columns = c(transactions, total_revenue, unique_buyers), fns = list(Total = ~ sum(., na.rm = TRUE)), fmt = list( ~ fmt_number(., columns = c(transactions, unique_buyers), decimals = 0), ~ fmt_currency(., columns = total_revenue, currency = "USD") ) ) |> opt_stylize(style = 1) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} revenue_daily |> ggplot(aes(x = event_date)) + geom_col( aes(y = total_revenue), fill = "#1565c0", width = 0.6, alpha = 0.85 ) + geom_line( aes(y = avg_order_value * (max(total_revenue) / max(avg_order_value))), colour = "#e65100", linewidth = 1.2 ) + geom_point( aes(y = avg_order_value * (max(total_revenue) / max(avg_order_value))), colour = "#e65100", size = 3 ) + scale_x_date(date_labels = "%a\n%b %d", date_breaks = "1 day") + scale_y_continuous( labels = dollar, name = "Total Revenue (bars)", sec.axis = sec_axis( trans = ~ . * (max(revenue_daily$avg_order_value) / max(revenue_daily$total_revenue)), name = "Avg. Order Value (line)", labels = dollar ) ) + labs( title = "Daily Revenue and Average Order Value", subtitle = "Bars = total revenue · Orange line = avg order value", x = NULL ) ``` ::: ## Analysis 5: Session Engagement Deep-Dive {#engagement} **Marketing Question**: Are sessions actually engaging, and which channels drive the most engaged sessions? This combines `event_params` extraction (Pattern 3) with struct unnesting (Pattern 1) in a single pipeline: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} session_engagement <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> mutate( # session_engaged comes from event_params (Pattern 3 — already flattened) is_engaged = session_engaged == "1", ga_session_num = as.integer(ga_session_number), # device_category from device struct (Pattern 1 — already flattened) device = str_to_title(device_category), # traffic_source_medium from traffic_source struct (Pattern 1) channel = case_when( is.na(traffic_source_medium) | traffic_source_medium == "(none)" ~ "Direct", traffic_source_medium == "organic" ~ "Organic Search", traffic_source_medium %in% c("cpc", "ppc", "paid") ~ "Paid Search", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "email") ~ "Email", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "social") ~ "Social", TRUE ~ str_to_title(traffic_source_medium) ) ) |> group_by(channel, device) |> summarise( sessions = n(), engaged_sessions = sum(is_engaged, na.rm = TRUE), engagement_rate = mean(is_engaged, na.rm = TRUE), new_user_sessions = sum(ga_session_num == 1, na.rm = TRUE), returning_sessions = sum(ga_session_num > 1, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop" ) |> filter(sessions >= 20) # Filter to statistically meaningful segments session_engagement ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} session_engagement |> ggplot(aes( x = device, y = fct_reorder(channel, engagement_rate), fill = engagement_rate )) + geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) + geom_text( aes(label = percent(engagement_rate, accuracy = 1)), fontface = "bold", size = 4 ) + scale_fill_gradient2( low = "#d32f2f", mid = "#fff176", high = "#388e3c", midpoint = 0.55, labels = percent, name = "Engagement\nRate" ) + labs( title = "Session Engagement Rate by Channel × Device", subtitle = glue( "Engagement rate = % of sessions meeting Google's engagement criteria\n", "(session_engaged parameter from event_params — Pattern 3 rectangling)" ), x = "Device Category", y = "Traffic Channel" ) + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 11) ) ``` ::: ::: callout-tip ## What is an "Engaged Session" in GA4? GA4 defines an engaged session as one that meets at least one of: - Lasts longer than 10 seconds - Has at least 2 page views or screen views - Has a conversion event The `session_engaged` parameter (value `"1"` or `"0"`) in `event_params` is how GA4 records this classification. We extracted it using the Pattern 3 pipeline from Part 2. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Rectangling Reference: Complete Cheat Sheet {#cheatsheet} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
GA4 Rectangling Cheat Sheet
Complete reference for every nested column type
GA4 Column BigQuery Type Pattern Pipeline1
device RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(device, names_sep='_')
geo RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(geo, names_sep='_')
traffic_source RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(traffic_source, names_sep='_')
app_info RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(app_info, names_sep='_')
user_ltv RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(user_ltv, names_sep='_')
ecommerce RECORD (struct) 1 unnest_wider(ecommerce, names_sep='_')
items RECORD REPEATED 2 unnest_longer(items) |> unnest_wider(items)
event_params RECORD REPEATED (KV) 3 unnest_longer() |> unnest_wider() ×2 |> coalesce() |> pivot_wider()
user_properties RECORD REPEATED (KV) 3 unnest_longer() |> unnest_wider() ×2 |> coalesce() |> pivot_wider()
1 Pattern 3 (key-value) is the only pattern not covered by R4DS Ch. 23. KV = Key-Value struct array.
``` ::: ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Exercises {#exercises} Work through these exercises to reinforce the concepts from this document. Each exercise is tagged with its relevant pattern and difficulty level. ::: callout-warning ## Exercise 1 — Pattern 1 · Beginner **Operating System Market Share** Using `unnest_wider()` on the `device` struct column, calculate the percentage of sessions from each operating system. Create a bar chart showing the top 8 operating systems. *Hint*: The field is `device$operating_system`. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show solution"} ga4_raw |> select(user_pseudo_id, event_name, device) |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> unnest_wider(device, names_sep = "_") |> count(device_operating_system) |> filter(!is.na(device_operating_system)) |> mutate( pct = n / sum(n), device_operating_system = fct_reorder(device_operating_system, n) ) |> slice_max(n, n = 8) |> ggplot(aes( x = device_operating_system, y = pct, fill = device_operating_system )) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.8 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + labs( title = "Sessions by Operating System", x = NULL, y = "Share of Sessions" ) ``` ::: ::: callout-warning ## Exercise 2 — Pattern 2 · Beginner **Average Items per Transaction** Using `unnest_longer()` + `unnest_wider()` on the `items` column, calculate: 1. The average number of line items per purchase transaction 2. The average `quantity` per line item 3. The top 5 item categories by total revenue *Hint*: Filter to `event_name == "purchase"` first. The category field is `item_category`. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show solution"} items_ex2 <- ga4_raw |> filter(event_name == "purchase") |> select(user_pseudo_id, event_timestamp, items) |> unnest_longer(items) |> unnest_wider(items) # Q1 & Q2 items_ex2 |> group_by(user_pseudo_id, event_timestamp) |> summarise( line_items = n(), total_qty = sum(quantity, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop" ) |> summarise( avg_line_items = mean(line_items), avg_quantity = mean(total_qty) ) # Q3 items_ex2 |> group_by(item_category) |> summarise( revenue = sum(item_revenue_in_usd, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop" ) |> filter(!is.na(item_category)) |> slice_max(revenue, n = 5) |> mutate(item_category = fct_reorder(item_category, revenue)) |> ggplot(aes(x = item_category, y = revenue, fill = item_category)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = dollar(revenue, accuracy = 1)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.8 ) + coord_flip() + scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") + labs( title = "Revenue by Item Category (Top 5)", x = NULL, y = "Total Revenue (USD)" ) ``` ::: ::: callout-warning ## Exercise 3 — Pattern 3 · Intermediate **Page Depth Analysis** Extract the `page_location` and `ga_session_id` parameters from `event_params`. For each session, count the number of page views. Then create a histogram showing the distribution of pages-per-session. *Questions to answer*: - What is the median number of pages per session? - What percentage of sessions view only a single page (bounce-like behaviour)? - Does desktop show higher page depth than mobile? ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show solution"} session_depth <- ga4_master |> filter( event_name == "page_view", !is.na(ga_session_id), !is.na(page_location) ) |> group_by(user_pseudo_id, ga_session_id, device_category) |> summarise(pages_viewed = n(), .groups = "drop") # Distribution stats session_depth |> summarise( median_pages = median(pages_viewed), single_page_pct = mean(pages_viewed == 1), avg_pages = mean(pages_viewed) ) # Histogram session_depth |> filter(pages_viewed <= 20) |> ggplot(aes(x = pages_viewed, fill = str_to_title(device_category))) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, position = "dodge", colour = "white") + scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:20) + scale_fill_manual( values = c( "Desktop" = "#4285F4", "Mobile" = "#34A853", "Tablet" = "#FBBC05" ) ) + labs( title = "Distribution of Pages Viewed per Session", x = "Pages per Session", y = "Number of Sessions", fill = "Device" ) ``` ::: ::: callout-warning ## Exercise 4 — All Patterns · Advanced **Cohort Conversion Analysis** This exercise combines all three rectangling patterns: 1. Use **Pattern 1** to extract ` traffic_source_medium` and `device_category` 2. Use **Pattern 3** to extract `ga_session_number` from `event_params` (identifies new vs. returning users) 3. Identify which users completed a purchase (`event_name == "purchase"`) 4. Build a summary table showing **conversion rate** broken down by: - New (session 1) vs. returning (session \> 1) - Traffic channel - Device category *Challenge*: Which new-user + channel combination has the highest conversion rate? What does that suggest about marketing strategy? ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show solution"} # Users and their session context user_sessions <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "session_start") |> mutate( user_type = if_else( as.integer(ga_session_number) == 1, "New User", "Returning User" ), channel = case_when( is.na(traffic_source_medium) | traffic_source_medium == "(none)" ~ "Direct", traffic_source_medium == "organic" ~ "Organic Search", traffic_source_medium %in% c("cpc", "ppc", "paid") ~ "Paid Search", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "email") ~ "Email", str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "social") ~ "Social", TRUE ~ str_to_title(traffic_source_medium) ), device = str_to_title(device_category) ) |> distinct(user_pseudo_id, user_type, channel, device) # Users who purchased purchasers <- ga4_master |> filter(event_name == "purchase") |> distinct(user_pseudo_id) |> mutate(purchased = TRUE) # Join and compute conversion rates user_sessions |> left_join(purchasers, by = "user_pseudo_id") |> mutate(purchased = replace_na(purchased, FALSE)) |> group_by(user_type, channel, device) |> summarise( users = n(), purchasers = sum(purchased), cvr = purchasers / users, .groups = "drop" ) |> filter(users >= 15) |> arrange(desc(cvr)) |> gt() |> tab_header( title = "Conversion Rate by User Type × Channel × Device", subtitle = "Minimum 15 users per segment" ) |> fmt_number(columns = c(users, purchasers), decimals = 0) |> fmt_percent(columns = cvr, decimals = 1) |> cols_label( user_type = "User Type", channel = "Channel", device = "Device", users = "Users", purchasers = "Purchasers", cvr = "CVR" ) |> data_color( columns = cvr, method = "numeric", palette = c("#ffffff", "#43a04720") ) |> opt_stylize(style = 1) ``` ::: ::: callout-warning ## Exercise 5 — Pattern 3 · Challenge **Build Your Own Reusable Pipeline Function** The `flatten_ga4_params()` helper introduced in Part 2 has a fragile ` tryCatch ` block to handle different `bigrquery` output formats. Rewrite it as a robust, well-documented function that: 1. Accepts any GA4 data frame and any key-value list-column name 2. Detects whether the `value` struct has already been expanded by checking column names 3. Handles NULL or zero-row list elements gracefully using `purrr::compact()` 4. Returns a wide tibble joined back to the original data (not just the params in isolation) 5. Includes input validation with informative error messages using `rlang::abort()` *Test it on both `event_params` and `user_properties`.* ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Summary {#summary} ## What We Covered This document extended R4DS Chapter 23 in two important directions: ### From R4DS (Applied to GA4) | Tool | GA4 Use Case | |------------------------|------------------------------------------------| | `unnest_wider()` | Flatten `device`, `geo`, `traffic_source`, `ecommerce` struct columns | | `unnest_longer()` | Expand `items` array and first-stage expansion of `event_params` | | `hoist()` | Fast targeted extraction of specific struct fields | | Both combined | Fully rectangling the `items` repeated struct array | ### Beyond R4DS (GA4-Specific) | Problem | Solution | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | Key-value struct arrays (`event_params`) | 3-stage pipeline: `unnest_longer()` → `unnest_wider()` ×2 → `coalesce()` → `pivot_wider()` | | Union-type value structs | `dplyr::coalesce()` across the four typed value columns | | BigQuery INT64 types | `bigint = "numeric"` in `bq_table_download()` | | Scale/performance | Download once → cache as `.qs` → load locally | ## The Three Patterns at a Glance ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Rectangling Function Reference
Steps 1a–1b cover R4DS Ch. 23 · Steps 3b–3c supplement it for GA4
Step Function Input Shape Output Shape
1a unnest_wider() list of named lists new atomic columns
1b hoist() list of named lists selected atomic columns
2 unnest_longer() list of tibbles (array) one row per array element
3a unnest_wider() one tibble-row per element named columns per field
3b coalesce() multiple typed value cols one unified value column
3c pivot_wider() long key-value rows one column per key
``` ::: ::: ## Further Reading | Resource | Topic | |------------------------------------------|------------------------------| | [R4DS Ch. 23](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/rectangling.html) | Full rectangling toolkit foundation | | [bigrquery docs](https://bigrquery.r-dbi.org/) | BigQuery connection and download options | | [GA4 BigQuery schema](https://support.google.com/analytics/answer/7029846) | Official field reference | | [tidyr vignette](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/rectangle.html) | Deep dive into rectangling functions | | [purrr cheat sheet](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/purrr.pdf) | List iteration patterns | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Reference - [R for Data Science, Ch23](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/rectangling.html)
